solution: Row-reducing \( A \) leaves \( [[answer]] \) nonzero (pivot) rows, so \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the nullity \( 4-\operatorname{rank}(A) \) (the number of free columns), not the rank itself.

stem: Let \( B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the nullity of \( B \) (the dimension of the solution set of \( B\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}\))?
solution: By the rank–nullity theorem, \( \text{nullity}(B) = 3 - \operatorname{rank}(B) \). Row-reducing shows \( \operatorname{rank}(B) = [[rankval]] \), so the nullity is \( 3 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank of \( B \), not the nullity; the two are swapped here.
This assumes \( B \) is the zero matrix (rank \(0\)), so every column would be free.
This overcounts the free columns by one.
This undercounts the free columns by one.
This is a plausible but unverified nullity value.
This is a plausible but unverified nullity value.
This assumes \( B \) is invertible (nullity \(0\)).

stem: Consider the homogeneous system of linear equations \[ \begin{aligned} w + [[s]]x + [[t]]y &= 0 \\ [[p]]w + [[m22]]x + [[m23]]y &= 0 \\ [[q]]w + [[m32]]x + [[m33]]y &= 0 \end{aligned} \] How many free parameters does the general solution have (i.e., what is the dimension of the solution space)?
solution: The number of free parameters equals the nullity of the coefficient matrix, \( 3 - \operatorname{rank} \). Row-reducing the coefficient matrix shows its rank is \( [[rankval]] \), so the solution space has dimension \( 3 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank of the coefficient matrix, not the number of free parameters.
This assumes every variable is free (the coefficient matrix is the zero matrix).
This overcounts the free parameters by one.
This undercounts the free parameters by one.
This is a plausible but unverified count.
This is a plausible but unverified count.
This assumes the only solution is the trivial one (the system has full rank).

stem: A linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \) has rank \( [[r]] \). By the rank–nullity theorem, what is the nullity of \( T \) (the dimension of its kernel)?
distractor_rationales: This uses the codomain dimension \( m \) instead of the domain dimension \( n \) (that formula gives the dimension of the left null space, not the kernel).
This reports the rank itself instead of subtracting it from the domain dimension.
This confuses the two given dimensions instead of applying the rank–nullity theorem.
This adds the rank instead of subtracting it.
This is off by one from the correct nullity.

This assumes the rank is \(0\) (T is the zero map).
